original_abstract: Per- and poly(fluoroalkyl) substances (PFAS) are environmentally persistent pollutants that are of growing concern due to their detrimental effects at ultratrace concentrations (ng·L-1) in human and environmental health. Suitable technologies for on-site ultratrace detection of PFAS do not exist and current methods require complex and specialized equipment, making the monitoring of PFAS in distributed water infrastructures extremely challenging. Herein, we describe amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) that can selectively detect perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) at concentrations of ng·L-1. The AFPs are highly fluorinated and have poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene backbones bearing pyridine-based selectors that react with acidic PFAS via a proton-transfer reaction. The fluorinated regions within the polymers partition PFAS into polymers, whereas the protonated pyridine units create lower-energy traps for the excitons, and emission from these pyridinium sites results in red-shifting of the fluorescence spectra. The AFPs are evaluated in thin-film and nanoparticle forms and can selectively detect PFAS concentrations of ∼1 ppb and ∼100 ppt, respectively. Both polymer films and nanoparticles are not affected by the type of water, and similar responses to PFAS were found in milliQ water, DI water, and well water. These results demonstrate a promising sensing approach for on-site detection of aqueous PFAS in the ng·L-1 range.
humanized_abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are ubiquitous contaminants that have been shown to cause adverse health effects in humans and wildlife at ultratrace concentrations (ng·L−1) in the environment. Their removal and characterization at these low concentrations is not currently possible using on-site or field-deployable remediation technologies due to their extremely low detection limits. Moreover, traditional laboratory techniques for analyzing PFAS, including liquid and gas chromatography and mass spectrometry, require sophisticated and expensive instrumentation and therefore make the monitoring of PFAS in large distributed water systems impractical. In this study, we synthesized the amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) that selectively detect perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) aqueous solutions in the ng·L−1 concentration range. The AFPs are highly fluorinated at their emission sites and contain a poly(p-phenylene ethynylene)/polyfluorene main chain with strategically placed pyridine functional groups that act as binding sites toward the protonizable PFAS (acidic) via a proton-transfer reaction. The highly fluorinated segment of the polymer confines and concentrates the detected PFAS within the polymer matrix, while the protonated sites of the pyridine act as the exciton traps. Upon binding of the target analyte, the resulting pyridinium species exhibit characteristic red-shifted fluorescence emissions. The AFPs were studied as both thin films and nanoscale (ca. 50 nm) particles. Both forms can detect PFAS at a minimum concentration of ∼1 ppb and a maximum concentration of ∼100 ppt. In addition, films and particles of AFPs showed similar fluorescence responses to aqueous PFAS in milliq, DI water, as well as well water.
